enrichment.short_description: Dover is a free-to-start applicant tracking system (ATS) with AI-powered resume scoring and note-taking, unlimited job board syndication, and an optional marketplace of fractional recruiters for startups and growing companies.
enrichment.company_size: Small team (reported employee counts range from roughly 15 to 130 depending on data source)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dover is a free applicant tracking system with AI candidate scoring and a fractional recruiter marketplace. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dover is a recruiting technology company that provides a free applicant tracking system (ATS) alongside an AI-driven candidate scoring engine and a marketplace of independent, fractional recruiters. The company was founded in San Francisco in 2019 by George Carollo, Max Kolysh, and Anvisha Pai, and it emerged from Y Combinator before raising a $20.1 million Series A round in July 2021 led by Tiger Global, with participation from Founders Fund, Abstract Ventures, and Y Combinator itself. Dover's stated mission is to make high-quality recruiting infrastructure accessible to companies that cannot afford a large in-house talent team, particularly venture-backed startups hiring their first few dozen employees.

At its core, Dover behaves like a conventional applicant tracking system: it lets a company post jobs, receive and organize applications, move candidates through customizable pipeline windows, and coordinate hiring-team feedback in one place. What differentiates it from legacy ATS incumbents is that the base product, called Free ATS, has no seat limits or job limits and is offered at no cost indefinitely, monetized instead through optional premium features and recruiter services rather than per-seat licensing. Job posts sync out to more than 100 external job boards automatically, and the platform integrates with Gmail and Outlook so recruiters can email candidates without leaving their inbox. On top of the free tier, Dover layers artificial intelligence: its AI Applicant Scoring feature reads incoming resumes against a role's requirements and ranks candidates, while AI Note Taking automatically summarizes recruiter and hiring-manager conversations with candidates so that feedback is captured consistently across a hiring loop.

Dover's paid tier, Premium ATS, costs 199 dollars per month and unlocks unlimited free basic job posts on LinkedIn and X (formerly Twitter), the AI scoring and note-taking features, and onboarding plus premium customer support. Beyond the ATS itself, Dover also runs a Recruiting Partner Marketplace, connecting companies with vetted, fractional (contract) recruiters who set their own rates; this lets an early-stage company access senior recruiting talent on a part-time or project basis without a full-time hire. Because recruiter rates in the marketplace are individually negotiated rather than published as a flat platform fee, that portion of Dover's business functions more like a marketplace commission than a subscription tier.

Dover's target users are primarily startup founders, early hiring managers, and small in-house talent teams — companies that are past relying on spreadsheets for recruiting but not yet large enough to justify the cost of enterprise ATS platforms like Greenhouse or SmartRecruiters. The free, unlimited-seat model is a deliberate wedge against competitors such as Lever, Ashby, Greenhouse, and Workable, all of which typically charge per recruiter seat or per open job from the first dollar. Dover's bet is that once a team's hiring workflow lives inside its free ATS, some fraction of customers will upgrade to Premium for the AI screening tools or tap the recruiter marketplace when they need extra hands during a hiring push.

A few notable facts stand out about Dover's story. It is one of the relatively small number of recruiting-technology startups to give away a genuinely unlimited free ATS tier rather than a time-limited trial, a positioning choice that has helped it accumulate a large base of small-company users. Its Series A was notable for bringing 'orchestration' language to recruiting software — Dover frames itself as coordinating the many moving pieces of a hiring process (sourcing, screening, interviewing, offer) rather than just tracking candidates passively. The founding team's backgrounds combine product, engineering, and recruiting-operations experience, and CEO George Carollo has continued to split time between customer-facing work and business operations as the company has scaled.

Compared with competitors, Dover is smaller and younger than category leaders like Greenhouse or Lever, and it does not disclose extensive enterprise features such as deep compliance tooling, custom SSO, or dedicated account management outside of Premium and marketplace customers. Its differentiation instead rests on cost (free for unlimited use), AI-assisted screening built into the base product experience, and the option to rent recruiting expertise on demand through its marketplace rather than requiring a company to hire an in-house recruiter outright. For companies that outgrow Dover's startup-oriented feature set, natural next steps in the market include Greenhouse, Ashby, or Lever, which offer more configurable enterprise workflows at a correspondingly higher price point.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dover is a San Francisco-based recruiting technology company offering a free applicant tracking system paired with AI-powered candidate scoring and an on-demand marketplace of fractional recruiters.

Founded in 2019 by George Carollo, Max Kolysh, and Anvisha Pai, and backed by a Y Combinator batch and a 2021 Series A led by Tiger Global, Dover positions itself as recruiting infrastructure for startups that cannot yet justify enterprise ATS pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Free ATS tier includes unlimited users and job listings, syndication to more than 100 job boards, Gmail and Outlook integration, and candidate pipeline tracking.
Premium ATS adds AI Applicant Scoring, which ranks incoming resumes against role requirements, and AI Note Taking, which summarizes interview and screening conversations automatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Dover's Free ATS plan costs nothing and has no seat or job limits, making it usable indefinitely by very small teams.

The Premium ATS plan is 199 dollars per month and unlocks unlimited free job posts on LinkedIn and X plus AI scoring, AI note-taking, and premium support. The Recruiting Marketplace operates separately, with independent fractional recruiters setting their own rates.

features.description: Premium subscribers get unlimited basic job listings on LinkedIn and X at no extra cost.

features.title: Recruiting Partner Marketplace
features.description: Connects companies with vetted, fractional recruiters who set their own rates for project-based hiring support.

features.description: Organizes applicants through customizable interview windows from application to offer.

Built-in AI resume scoring and note-taking reduce manual screening work
Access to a marketplace of fractional recruiters without a full-time hire
Strong job board reach with over 100 integrations from a single post
cons: AI scoring and note-taking are gated behind the 199 dollars per month Premium plan
Smaller company than category leaders, so fewer deep integrations and enterprise controls
Recruiting Marketplace rates are set individually by recruiters, adding variable cost
Best suited to early-stage startups; less proven at large enterprise hiring scale

faqs.question: Is Dover free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dover's Free ATS plan has no cost and no limits on users or job postings; paid features are optional add-ons.

faqs.question: How much does Dover's Premium ATS cost?
faqs.answer: Premium ATS is 199 dollars per month and adds AI applicant scoring, AI note-taking, unlimited free LinkedIn and X job posts, and premium support.

faqs.question: What is the Dover Recruiting Marketplace?
faqs.answer: It is a network of independent, fractional recruiters that Dover customers can hire on a project basis; recruiters set their own rates.

faqs.question: Who founded Dover and when?
faqs.answer: Dover was founded in 2019 in San Francisco by George Carollo, Max Kolysh, and Anvisha Pai.

faqs.question: Does Dover use artificial intelligence?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Premium tier includes AI-based resume scoring against job requirements and AI-generated summaries of candidate conversations.

faqs.question: Who are Dover's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Dover competes with ATS platforms such as Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby, Workable, and Gem, most of which charge per-seat fees from the outset.

enrichment.short_description: Dovetail is a customer insights and user research platform that centralizes interviews, surveys, and feedback into a searchable repository with AI-powered summaries, tagging, and analysis.
enrichment.headquarters: Sydney, Australia (with a major hub in San Francisco, California)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dovetail centralizes user research and customer feedback with AI summaries and tagging. Compare 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dovetail is a customer intelligence and user research platform designed to help product, design, and research teams capture, organize, and analyze qualitative feedback in one searchable repository. The company was founded in 2017 in Sydney, Australia, by Benjamin Humphrey and Bradley Ayers, both former Atlassian employees. Humphrey left Atlassian in 2017 to start the company and recruited Ayers, his former colleague and friend, as co-founder. Dovetail has since grown into a company with hubs in Sydney and San Francisco, backed by investors including Blackbird Ventures, and has become one of the most widely referenced tools in the user-research and product-discovery space.

At a technical level, Dovetail functions as a repository and analysis layer that sits on top of a team's existing research activities. Teams upload interview recordings, call transcripts, survey responses, support tickets, sales calls, and other unstructured customer feedback into Dovetail 'channels' — automated ingestion windows that pull data in from tools like Zoom, Gong, Intercom, and Zendesk. Dovetail then applies AI to transcribe recordings, generate summaries, and surface recurring themes, while also letting researchers manually tag, highlight, and annotate raw data. The platform's AI chat interface lets users ask natural-language questions about the whole corpus of research (for example, 'what do enterprise customers say about onboarding') and receive synthesized answers with citations back to the underlying source clips, effectively turning scattered qualitative data into a queryable knowledge base. Dovetail also supports structured research artifacts such as personas, journey maps, and shareable 'Docs' that combine findings with supporting evidence for the rest of the organization.

Dovetail's pricing follows a freemium model built around per-seat licensing plus usage-based add-ons. The Free plan costs nothing and requires no credit card, but it is capped at one project and one data-ingestion channel, with basic AI summaries and AI chat included. Paid tiers scale from there: independent third-party pricing trackers report a Professional-equivalent tier priced in the neighborhood of 29 to 39 dollars per user per month when billed annually, unlocking unlimited projects, deeper AI analysis, more integrations, and higher transcription and storage limits. At the top, Dovetail offers a custom-quoted Enterprise plan that is invoiced rather than self-served, adding unlimited AI agents, unlimited channels, AI dashboards, single sign-on, audit logs, custom data retention, a HIPAA add-on for regulated industries, and a dedicated customer success manager. Because pricing is per seat, cost scales with how many researchers, designers, and stakeholders need access; third-party estimates put a five-person research team on a mid-tier plan at roughly 150 to 250 dollars per month before usage-based Channels add-ons, which themselves start around 50 dollars per month for additional automated data ingestion.

Dovetail's core users are user researchers, product designers, and product managers at technology companies who need to make qualitative customer feedback searchable and shareable across an organization, rather than trapped in individual researchers' notebooks or disconnected transcripts. It is also increasingly used by customer success and sales teams who want product and research organizations to have visibility into what customers are saying in calls and tickets, not just in formal research studies. The platform's emphasis on AI-assisted synthesis is aimed at solving a well-known bottleneck in research operations: the time it takes a human to manually code and theme large volumes of qualitative data.

In the customer insights and research repository market, Dovetail's most direct competitors include Condens, EnjoyHQ, Marvin, UserTesting, and, at the lighter-weight end, general-purpose tools like Notion or Airtable repurposed for research storage. Dovetail differentiates itself through its AI-native search and synthesis layer, its channel-based automated ingestion from call-recording and support tools, and its focus specifically on being a repository of record for qualitative insight rather than a tool for running studies (unlike UserTesting, which also recruits and manages research participants). A notable aspect of Dovetail's growth story is that it built a strong following among design and research communities organically, in part through free educational content and templates, before layering in enterprise security features like SSO and HIPAA support to move upmarket into larger, more regulated customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dovetail is a research and customer insights repository founded in Sydney, Australia in 2017 by former Atlassian colleagues Benjamin Humphrey and Bradley Ayers.
The platform ingests interviews, transcripts, surveys, and support tickets, then uses AI to summarize and surface themes across an organization's qualitative customer data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Automated data ingestion channels pull recordings and tickets in from tools like Zoom, Gong, Intercom, and Zendesk, while AI transcribes and tags the content.
An AI chat interface lets teams ask natural-language questions across their entire research corpus and get synthesized, source-linked answers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Dovetail's Free plan supports one project and one channel with basic AI summaries and no credit card required.

Paid per-seat tiers (reported around 29 to 39 dollars per user per month annually) unlock unlimited projects and deeper AI features, while Enterprise is custom-quoted and adds SSO, audit logs, and a HIPAA add-on.

features.description: Combine findings, quotes, and clips into shareable documents for stakeholders outside the research team.

pros: Strong AI-assisted synthesis reduces manual coding of qualitative data
Automated channels keep the repository updated without manual uploads
Free plan lets small teams or individuals try the product with no card required
Enterprise-grade security options support regulated industries like healthcare

Free plan is limited to a single project and a single ingestion channel
Enterprise pricing is custom and not published, requiring a sales conversation
Usage-based add-ons like extra Channels add further cost on top of seat fees

faqs.question: Is Dovetail free to use?
faqs.answer: Dovetail offers a Free plan with one project, one data channel, basic AI summaries, and no credit card required; deeper functionality requires a paid plan.

faqs.question: How much does Dovetail cost per user?
faqs.answer: Third-party pricing trackers report a mid-tier plan around 29 to 39 dollars per user per month when billed annually; Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted.

faqs.question: Who founded Dovetail?
faqs.answer: Dovetail was founded in 2017 in Sydney, Australia by Benjamin Humphrey and Bradley Ayers, both former Atlassian employees.

faqs.question: What does Dovetail do with AI?
faqs.answer: It transcribes recordings, generates summaries, tags recurring themes, and powers an AI chat interface that answers questions across a team's full research repository.

faqs.question: Is Dovetail HIPAA compliant?
faqs.answer: Dovetail offers a HIPAA add-on as part of its Enterprise plan for organizations handling regulated health data.

faqs.question: What are Dovetail's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Dovetail competes with research repository and insights tools such as Condens, EnjoyHQ, Marvin, and UserTesting.

use_cases.title: Sharing findings across the organization
use_cases.description: A research team builds shareable Docs with quotes and clips so sales and leadership can see evidence behind product decisions.

enrichment.short_description: Downtify is a free, open-source, self-hosted app that lets you download Spotify playlists, albums, and tracks as tagged audio files by matching them to YouTube Music, run entirely in a single Docker container.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Downtify is a free, self-hosted, open-source Spotify downloader. Read about its features, Docker setup, supported formats, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Downtify is a free, open-source, self-hosted application that downloads music referenced by Spotify links — individual tracks, albums, or entire playlists — and saves them locally as properly tagged audio files with embedded album art. It is created and maintained by a Brazilian developer who goes by henriquesebastiao, and the project is hosted on GitHub under the GPL-3.0 license. The public repository was created in February 2025, and the project has continued to see regular monthly releases through 2026, with hundreds of GitHub stars reflecting adoption in the self-hosting and home-lab community.

Technically, Downtify does not use the official Spotify API or require a Spotify Premium account or any API credentials. Instead, it reads the metadata that Spotify already exposes publicly on its embed pages for a given playlist, album, or track — artist names, track titles, album art, and ordering. Downtify then searches YouTube Music for the closest matching audio for each track, hands the resulting video off to yt-dlp to extract audio, uses ffmpeg to transcode it into the user's chosen format, and finally applies proper ID3 or equivalent metadata tags using the Python library mutagen. The application is built with a Python backend and a Vue.js frontend, communicates download progress to the browser in real time over WebSockets, and ships as a single Docker image, making it straightforward to deploy on a home server or NAS alongside other self-hosted media tools.

Download output supports five audio formats — MP3, FLAC, M4A, OGG, and OPUS — at a bitrate the user selects, and the tool can optionally organize downloaded files into folders by artist. A standout feature is the Playlist Monitor, which periodically checks a saved Spotify playlist and automatically downloads any newly added tracks without requiring the user to re-trigger anything, effectively turning a Spotify playlist into a self-updating local music library. Downtify also embeds lyrics — both plain text and time-synced (karaoke-style) lyrics sourced from the lrclib service — directly into the downloaded files. Beyond downloading, Downtify includes a built-in web-based music player with shuffle, repeat, and volume controls so users can listen to their downloaded library without a separate media app, and it can generate standard EXTM3U playlist files compatible with self-hosted media servers such as Jellyfin, Navidrome, and Plex. The interface is available in English, Spanish, and Brazilian Portuguese.

Because Downtify has no company, funding, or commercial pricing behind it, there is no pricing model to speak of beyond 'free and self-hosted' — users run their own instance and bear their own infrastructure costs (typically negligible, since it runs comfortably on a small home server). It is distributed exclusively as source code and as a prebuilt Docker image on GitHub Container Registry, with community packaging available for popular home-server app stores such as Umbrel, CasaOS, and HomeDock, which lets less technical users install it with a few clicks rather than a manual Docker command.

Downtify's target users are self-hosting enthusiasts, home-lab operators, and privacy-conscious listeners who want a personal, offline copy of music they already have access to via Spotify playlists, without depending on a third-party cloud service or requiring a Spotify Premium subscription. It sits in the same broad category as other community-built Spotify-to-local-file tools, such as spotDL and various forks including a project called downtify-premium built by another developer on top of the same idea, but Downtify differentiates itself with its polished built-in web player, its Playlist Monitor auto-sync feature, and its single-container Docker deployment model that fits cleanly into a self-hosted media stack. As with any tool that downloads copyrighted audio from streaming platforms, users are responsible for ensuring their use complies with Spotify's terms of service and applicable copyright law in their jurisdiction; the project itself is offered purely as open-source software with no warranty or affiliation with Spotify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Downtify is an open-source, self-hosted tool that converts Spotify playlists, albums, and tracks into locally stored, properly tagged audio files.
Created by developer henriquesebastiao and released under the GPL-3.0 license, it runs entirely inside a single Docker container with no Spotify API keys or Premium subscription required.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: It reads public Spotify metadata, finds a matching track on YouTube Music, and processes audio through yt-dlp and ffmpeg before tagging files with mutagen.
Notable features include a Playlist Monitor for auto-downloading new tracks, five supported audio formats, embedded synced lyrics, and a built-in web music player.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Downtify is completely free and open source; there are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees.
Users deploy their own instance via Docker or Docker Compose, or through community packages for home-server platforms like Umbrel, CasaOS, and HomeDock.

features.title: Built-in web music player

pros: Completely free, open-source, and self-hosted with no subscription
No Spotify API credentials or Premium account needed to use it
Playlist Monitor keeps a local library automatically in sync with a Spotify playlist
Simple one-command Docker deployment plus home-server app store packages
cons: Audio comes from YouTube Music matches rather than Spotify's actual audio streams, so match quality can vary
Requires self-hosting knowledge and your own server or home-lab infrastructure
Maintained by a single independent developer rather than a company, with no formal support guarantees
Downloading copyrighted content may conflict with Spotify's terms of service depending on jurisdiction and use

faqs.question: Is Downtify free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Downtify is fully free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, with no subscription or paid tier.

faqs.question: Do I need a Spotify Premium account to use Downtify?
faqs.answer: No. Downtify reads Spotify's public embed metadata and does not require a Premium account or API credentials.

faqs.question: What audio formats does Downtify support?
faqs.answer: MP3, FLAC, M4A, OGG, and OPUS, each at a bitrate you choose.

faqs.question: How do I install Downtify?
faqs.answer: Run its official Docker image with a single command, use Docker Compose, or install it via community packages for Umbrel, CasaOS, or HomeDock.

faqs.question: What is Downtify's Playlist Monitor?
faqs.answer: A feature that periodically checks a saved playlist and automatically downloads any newly added tracks.

faqs.question: Who maintains Downtify?
faqs.answer: It is built and maintained by an independent developer, henriquesebastiao, with the source code publicly hosted on GitHub.

use_cases.description: A user sets up Playlist Monitor on a collaborative playlist so every newly added track is automatically downloaded and tagged.

features: Unlimited downloads on your own infrastructure
All audio formats and features included

enrichment.short_description: Dragonboat is a strategic product portfolio management platform that connects company strategy, roadmaps, and outcomes with AI-assisted tracking across tools like Jira, Azure DevOps, Asana, and Slack.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dragonboat is a strategic product portfolio management platform with AI tracking and roadmap tools. See 2026 features, pricing tiers, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dragonboat is a strategic product portfolio management (SPPM) platform built to help product organizations connect company strategy, resource allocation, and roadmaps with measurable business outcomes. The company was founded in 2018 in Santa Clara, California, by Becky Flint, who serves as CEO, alongside co-founder Lee Bailey. Dragonboat has raised roughly 15 million dollars in total funding, beginning with a 3 million dollar seed round from Act One Ventures, Roble Ventures, and GingerBread Capital, followed by a 12 million dollar Series A that added Insight Partners as a lead investor alongside the earlier seed backers.

Unlike single-team roadmapping tools, Dragonboat is explicitly positioned for portfolio-level product management: it is designed for VPs of product, Chief Product Officers, and product operations teams who need to see and prioritize work across many product lines or business units simultaneously, rather than just one team's backlog. The platform organizes work around a hierarchy that links company strategy and objectives down through outcomes, initiatives, and features, then rolls execution data back up so leadership can see whether day-to-day engineering work is actually advancing stated strategic goals. A defining feature of the current product is what Dragonboat calls its Ambient Agent, an AI capability that automatically tracks and reports on progress across the product development lifecycle by pulling status updates from connected tools such as Jira, Azure DevOps, Asana, and Slack, reducing the manual status-update overhead that traditionally burdens product managers in portfolio reviews.

Dragonboat's other core capabilities include portfolio-wide roadmap building that visualizes multiple teams' work on a single timeline, customer insight and feedback aggregation to inform prioritization, and configurable prioritization frameworks that weigh strategic fit, effort, and expected outcome. For larger organizations, Dragonboat supports complex, multi-level organizational hierarchies, advanced integrations and data transformation between connected systems, portfolio trend analysis that can flag risks and dependencies before they cause delays, and enterprise security controls including two-factor authentication and single sign-on via Microsoft Entra ID or Okta. The company also sells a set of optional 'Advanced Apps' as add-on modules — an Advanced Strategy App, Advanced Idea Management, an Advanced Resource Planner, and an Advanced PDLC (product development lifecycle) App — that extend specific parts of the base platform for teams with more sophisticated needs.

Dragonboat's pricing is not published on its website; both its Starter and Enterprise plans require contacting sales for a quote, which is typical for portfolio-level enterprise software sold primarily to mid-market and large product organizations. The Starter tier is aimed at smaller teams with a simpler organizational structure and includes the Ambient Agent integrations, strategy-to-roadmap connections, customer insight tools, and free read-only or requestor-level access for up to 100 additional users, letting a broader set of stakeholders view or submit ideas without each needing a full paid seat. The Enterprise tier builds on Starter with support for intricate, multi-level organizational hierarchies, custom configurations for enterprise systems, predictive portfolio trend and risk analysis, deeper integrations, stronger security controls, and a dedicated customer success manager.

Dragonboat competes in the product management platform category most directly with Productboard, Aha!, and Jira Product Discovery, as well as broader roadmapping tools like Airfocus and ProductPlan. Its differentiation is its explicit focus on the portfolio and strategy layer — connecting objectives and key results to a multi-team roadmap and then to execution data pulled automatically from delivery tools — rather than being primarily a single-team feedback-and-roadmap tool. This makes Dragonboat particularly relevant for organizations managing several product lines, business units, or a complex matrix of teams who need one shared view of how work maps to strategy, as opposed to smaller product teams that may be better served by a lighter-weight, single-team roadmapping tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dragonboat is a Santa Clara, California-based strategic product portfolio management platform founded in 2018 by Becky Flint and Lee Bailey.
It connects company strategy and objectives to multi-team roadmaps and execution data, targeting product leaders who manage several product lines or business units at once.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Its Ambient Agent uses AI to automatically track and report on product development progress by pulling data from Jira, Azure DevOps, Asana, and Slack.
The platform supports portfolio-wide roadmapping, customer insight aggregation, configurable prioritization frameworks, and predictive risk and dependency analysis for enterprise customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Dragonboat does not publish fixed prices; both its Starter and Enterprise plans are quoted after contacting sales.

Starter includes free read-only or requestor access for up to 100 additional users, while Enterprise adds complex hierarchy support, advanced security, and a dedicated customer success manager. Optional Advanced Apps are sold separately.

features.description: Add-on modules for advanced strategy, idea management, resource planning, and product development lifecycle tracking.

pros: Purpose-built for portfolio-level strategy, not just single-team roadmaps
AI Ambient Agent reduces manual status-update work by pulling data from delivery tools
Free read-only or requestor access for up to 100 users lowers the cost of broad stakeholder visibility
Strong support for complex, multi-level organizational hierarchies at the Enterprise tier
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation for both tiers
Advanced capabilities like predictive risk analysis are reserved for the Enterprise plan
Some deeper functionality is sold as separate paid Advanced Apps rather than included
Best suited to multi-team or multi-product organizations; may be more than a single small product team needs

faqs.question: How much does Dragonboat cost?
faqs.answer: Dragonboat does not publish fixed pricing; both its Starter and Enterprise plans require contacting sales for a custom quote.

faqs.question: Who founded Dragonboat?
faqs.answer: Dragonboat was founded in 2018 in Santa Clara, California, by CEO Becky Flint and co-founder Lee Bailey.

faqs.question: What is Dragonboat's Ambient Agent?
faqs.answer: It is an AI feature that automatically tracks and reports on product development progress by pulling status data from tools like Jira, Azure DevOps, Asana, and Slack.

faqs.question: How much funding has Dragonboat raised?
faqs.answer: Dragonboat has raised approximately 15 million dollars, including a 3 million dollar seed round and a 12 million dollar Series A led by Insight Partners.

faqs.question: Who is Dragonboat designed for?
faqs.answer: It is designed for VPs of product, Chief Product Officers, and product operations teams managing multiple product lines or business units.

faqs.question: What are Dragonboat's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Dragonboat competes with Productboard, Aha!, Jira Product Discovery, and other roadmapping and product portfolio tools.

use_cases.title: Aligning multi-team roadmaps with strategy

use_cases.title: Automating portfolio status reporting

use_cases.title: Prioritizing across business units

features: Ambient Agent integrations with Jira, ADO, Asana, Slack
Strategy and outcomes connected to roadmaps
Free read-only and requestor access for up to 100 users
Support for complex, multi-level organizational hierarchies
Two-factor authentication and SSO (Microsoft Entra ID / Okta)

enrichment.short_description: Dragonfly is a high-performance, in-memory data store built as a drop-in replacement for Redis and Memcached, offered as source-available software you can self-host or as a fully managed cloud service.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source / Freemium (self-hosted free; managed cloud is paid)
enrichment.headquarters: Tel Aviv, Israel and San Francisco, California, USA (dual headquarters)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dragonfly is a Redis-compatible, in-memory data store you can self-host free or run as a managed cloud service. See 2026 pricing, features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dragonfly, commonly known by its product name DragonflyDB, is an in-memory data store designed as a modern, drop-in replacement for Redis and Memcached. It was founded in March 2022 by Roman Gershman and Oded Poncz, two engineers who previously worked together at Google and who built Dragonfly after repeatedly running into the scaling limitations of Redis in production. The company has dual headquarters in Tel Aviv, Israel and San Francisco, California, with team members distributed globally. Dragonfly raised 21 million dollars in combined seed and Series A funding, announced together in March 2023, with the seed round led by Redpoint Ventures and the Series A led by Quiet Capital; that funding round coincided with Dragonfly reaching general availability.

Technically, Dragonfly is a from-scratch rewrite rather than a fork of Redis: it is implemented in C++ using a shared-nothing, multi-threaded architecture that lets it scale vertically across all of a machine's CPU cores, in contrast to Redis's traditionally single-threaded core design. Despite this different internal architecture, Dragonfly implements the same wire protocol as Redis (both RESP2 and RESP3) and, as of recent releases, supports more than 240 Redis-compatible commands, which means existing Redis client libraries, SDKs, and GUI management tools can generally connect to Dragonfly without code changes. This compatibility-first approach is the core of Dragonfly's pitch: teams already using Redis for caching, session storage, rate limiting, pub/sub messaging, or as a lightweight database can swap in Dragonfly and, in many benchmarked workloads, get substantially higher throughput and memory efficiency on the same hardware, without rewriting application code.

Dragonfly's source code is publicly available on GitHub, where the project has accumulated tens of thousands of stars, but it is released under the Business Source License 1.1 (BSL) rather than a traditional OSI-approved open-source license. Under the BSL, the code is free to use, modify, and self-host for nearly any purpose, including inside your own product, with one key restriction: you may not offer Dragonfly itself as a competing hosted database-as-a-service. The license is time-limited by design — each version of the code automatically converts to the fully permissive Apache License 2.0 after a set number of years (specific versions convert to Apache 2.0 by March 2029 under the current terms) — a licensing pattern several infrastructure companies have adopted specifically to prevent large cloud providers from repackaging their software as a competing managed service while still keeping the code publicly readable and self-hostable today.

For teams who would rather not operate Dragonfly themselves, the company sells Dragonfly Cloud, a fully managed service available on AWS, GCP, and Azure. Pricing on Dragonfly Cloud starts with a Flex plan aimed at self-serve teams, beginning around 36 dollars per month with one-click provisioning and horizontal and vertical scaling; a Business plan aimed at teams needing advanced security, compliance, and support starts around 2,000 dollars per month and adds bring-your-own-cloud deployment, auto-scaling, SSO and multi-factor authentication, and custom service-level agreements; and a top Enterprise tier is custom-priced for large organizations requiring air-gapped infrastructure, custom instance types, and white-glove migrations. Dragonfly Cloud also publishes an on-demand consumption rate of roughly 8 dollars per gigabyte of memory per month, with committed-use discounts available, and the company markets memory efficiency gains of around 20 percent compared with equivalent Redis deployments.

Dragonfly's target users are backend engineering and infrastructure teams operating caching layers, session stores, real-time leaderboards, rate limiters, or pub/sub systems at meaningful scale, where Redis's single-threaded architecture becomes a bottleneck. Its most direct competitors are Redis itself (including Redis's own managed cloud offering), KeyDB, Valkey (the Linux Foundation-backed open-source fork created after Redis changed its licensing), and Memcached for simpler caching use cases. Dragonfly differentiates itself primarily on raw performance and memory efficiency per server, protocol-level compatibility that minimizes migration effort, and offering both a genuinely self-hostable core product and a managed cloud alternative, giving teams a choice between running it themselves for free or paying for a fully operated service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dragonfly (DragonflyDB) is an in-memory data store built as a high-performance, drop-in replacement for Redis and Memcached, founded in 2022 by former Google engineers Roman Gershman and Oded Poncz.
The company is dual-headquartered in Tel Aviv and San Francisco and raised 21 million dollars in combined seed and Series A funding led by Redpoint Ventures and Quiet Capital.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Written from scratch in C++ with a shared-nothing, multi-threaded architecture, Dragonfly scales across all CPU cores while remaining wire-compatible with Redis's RESP2 and RESP3 protocols and over 240 Redis commands.

The source code is publicly available under the Business Source License 1.1, which permits free self-hosting and modification but restricts offering Dragonfly itself as a competing hosted database service; the license converts to Apache 2.0 after a set number of years.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Self-hosting Dragonfly is free under its source-available license.

Dragonfly Cloud, the managed service, starts with a self-serve Flex plan around 36 dollars per month, a Business plan around 2,000 dollars per month for advanced security and support, and custom Enterprise pricing for air-gapped deployments, with on-demand usage priced around 8 dollars per gigabyte of memory per month.

features.title: Redis-protocol compatibility
features.description: Supports RESP2 and RESP3 and over 240 Redis commands, so existing Redis clients and tools work without code changes.

features.title: Multi-threaded, shared-nothing architecture
features.description: Written in C++ to scale across all available CPU cores, unlike Redis's traditionally single-threaded core.

features.title: Memcached compatibility
features.description: Can also serve as a drop-in replacement for Memcached-based caching layers.

features.title: Source-available under BSL 1.1
features.description: Code is free to self-host and modify, with a restriction against offering it as a competing hosted database service.

features.title: Fully managed Dragonfly Cloud
features.description: One-click provisioning on AWS, GCP, or Azure with horizontal and vertical scaling and a monitoring dashboard.

features.title: Bring-your-own-cloud deployment
features.description: Business and Enterprise tiers let customers run Dragonfly Cloud inside their own cloud account.

features.title: Enterprise security controls
features.description: SSO, multi-factor authentication, custom SLAs, and air-gapped infrastructure support at higher tiers.

features.title: Improved memory efficiency
features.description: Marketed memory efficiency gains of around 20 percent compared with equivalent Redis deployments.

pros: Free to self-host with source code publicly available on GitHub
Drop-in compatibility with existing Redis clients and tooling minimizes migration work
Multi-threaded architecture delivers higher throughput per server than single-threaded Redis in many workloads
Offers both a self-hosted path and a fully managed cloud option for teams that want either
cons: Business Source License 1.1 is not an OSI-approved open-source license and restricts competing hosted offerings
Managed Business tier starts around 2,000 dollars per month, a steep jump from the self-serve Flex tier
Younger company (founded 2022) with a smaller ecosystem and community than long-established Redis
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a direct sales conversation

faqs.question: Is Dragonfly open source?
faqs.answer: Dragonfly's source code is publicly available on GitHub under the Business Source License 1.1, which allows free self-hosting and modification but is not an OSI-approved open-source license; it converts to Apache 2.0 after a set number of years.

faqs.question: Is Dragonfly compatible with Redis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dragonfly implements the RESP2 and RESP3 protocols and over 240 Redis commands, so most existing Redis clients and tools work without modification.

faqs.question: How much does Dragonfly Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Dragonfly Cloud starts with a Flex plan around 36 dollars per month, a Business plan around 2,000 dollars per month, and custom Enterprise pricing for large organizations.

faqs.question: Who founded Dragonfly?
faqs.answer: Dragonfly was founded in March 2022 by former Google engineers Roman Gershman and Oded Poncz.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Dragonfly for free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dragonfly can be self-hosted at no cost under its source-available license; Dragonfly Cloud is the paid, fully managed alternative.

faqs.question: What are Dragonfly's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Dragonfly competes with Redis, Redis's own managed cloud, KeyDB, Valkey, and Memcached.

use_cases.title: Replacing Redis for higher throughput
use_cases.description: A backend team migrates an existing Redis caching layer to Dragonfly to get higher throughput per server without changing client code.

use_cases.title: Managed in-memory data store for production apps
use_cases.description: A startup uses Dragonfly Cloud's Flex plan to get a fully managed, auto-scaling cache without operating infrastructure themselves.

features: Full Redis and Memcached protocol compatibility
Fully-managed on AWS, GCP, or Azure

enrichment.short_description: Drata is an automated compliance and trust management platform that continuously monitors controls and evidence for frameworks like SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, and GDPR to streamline security audits.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing, typically starting around $7,500-$15,000/year (third-party estimates)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Drata automates SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA compliance monitoring. See 2026 pricing estimates, features, pros, cons, and how it compares to Vanta.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Drata is a compliance automation and trust management platform that helps companies achieve and continuously maintain certifications and attestations such as SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and GDPR. The company was founded in 2020 in San Diego, California, by Adam Markowitz, Daniel Marashlian, and Troy Markowitz. Drata grew quickly during the surge in demand for compliance tooling that followed increased enterprise scrutiny of vendor security, and it has raised a total of 328 million dollars across four funding rounds, most notably a 200 million dollar Series C in December 2022 that valued the company at 2 billion dollars. Drata now reports more than 100 million dollars in annual recurring revenue and serves upward of 7,000 corporate customers across roughly 60 countries, including well-known technology companies such as Notion, OpenAI, Okta, SentinelOne, and CrowdStrike.

The core problem Drata solves is that preparing for a security audit or compliance certification traditionally requires large amounts of manual evidence collection: screenshots of configuration settings, exported access logs, spreadsheets tracking policy acknowledgments, and repeated coordination between security, IT, HR, and engineering teams, often redone from scratch for every audit cycle. Drata automates this by connecting directly to a company's existing technology stack — cloud infrastructure providers such as AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud, identity providers, HR systems, version control, ticketing tools, and more — and continuously pulling in evidence and monitoring the state of security controls in near real time. Rather than a point-in-time snapshot, Drata is designed to flag control drift (for example, an employee who should have been offboarded but still has system access) as soon as it happens, so a company stays audit-ready year-round instead of scrambling before each renewal. The platform maps this continuous evidence to the specific control requirements of whichever frameworks a company is pursuing, and it works directly with a network of more than 2,500 independent auditors that Drata has vetted, letting customers run their actual audit through partners connected to the platform.

Drata does not publish its pricing publicly, which is typical for compliance software sold on annual contracts sized to company complexity, framework count, and integration needs. Third-party pricing analyses and cost benchmarking sites report roughly three tiers: an entry-level tier often referred to as Essential priced around 7,500 dollars per year for a single framework and a smaller company, a mid-tier Foundation package around 15,000 dollars per year, and a top Advanced tier that is custom-priced for larger organizations pursuing multiple frameworks simultaneously. Analysts estimate that a typical multi-framework customer ends up spending somewhere between 15,000 and 25,000 dollars annually, with average contract values reported around 13,500 dollars, though enterprise customers with more frameworks, more integrations, and more employees can pay considerably more, with some benchmarks citing costs up to roughly 100,000 dollars per year at the high end.

Drata's primary users are security, compliance, IT, and engineering leaders at technology companies — particularly venture-backed SaaS startups and scale-ups — who need to pass enterprise customers' vendor security reviews or meet regulatory requirements, but who do not have a large dedicated compliance team to manage the process manually. It is also used by the auditors themselves, since Drata's platform gives them direct, structured access to evidence rather than requiring email threads and spreadsheet exports. Because compliance certifications like SOC 2 are frequently a prerequisite for closing enterprise sales deals, Drata is often adopted specifically to unblock revenue rather than purely as a security investment, which has helped drive its rapid customer growth.

In the compliance automation market, Drata's closest and most frequently compared competitor is Vanta, with Secureframe, Sprinto, Tugboat Logic (now OneTrust), and Thoropass also competing for similar customers. Drata differentiates itself with the breadth of its auditor network, the depth of its framework coverage, its continuous control monitoring approach versus periodic manual checks, and features aimed at giving security teams a single 'trust management' system of record that can also power public-facing trust pages showing customers and prospects a company's real-time security posture. Its rapid fundraising and unicorn valuation reflect how central automated, continuous compliance monitoring has become to B2B SaaS sales cycles since the early 2020s.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Drata is a San Diego-based compliance automation platform founded in 2020 by Adam Markowitz, Daniel Marashlian, and Troy Markowitz.

It has raised 328 million dollars in total funding, including a 200 million dollar Series C in December 2022 at a 2 billion dollar valuation, and reports over 100 million dollars in annual recurring revenue serving more than 7,000 customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Drata connects to a company's cloud infrastructure, identity, HR, and engineering tools to continuously collect evidence and monitor security controls in near real time.
It maps continuous evidence to frameworks including SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and GDPR, and connects customers to a network of more than 2,500 vetted independent auditors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Drata does not publish pricing; it sells custom annual contracts sized to a company's frameworks, integrations, and headcount.

Third-party benchmarks report roughly three tiers, from an entry Essential tier around 7,500 dollars per year to a Foundation tier around 15,000 dollars per year, up to a custom-priced Advanced tier for large, multi-framework customers.

features.title: Continuous control monitoring
features.description: Automatically flags control drift, such as unrevoked access after an employee offboarding, as it happens rather than at audit time.

features.title: Automated evidence collection
features.description: Pulls evidence directly from connected cloud, identity, HR, and engineering tools instead of manual screenshots and spreadsheets.

features.title: Multi-framework support
features.description: Maps a single set of evidence to multiple frameworks including SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, PCI DSS, and GDPR.

features.title: Vetted auditor network
features.description: Connects customers to more than 2,500 independent auditors who can review evidence directly inside the platform.

features.title: Public trust pages
features.description: Lets companies show customers and prospects a real-time view of their security and compliance posture.

features.title: Policy and risk management
features.description: Centralizes security policies, risk assessments, and employee acknowledgments in one system of record.

features.title: Vendor risk management
features.description: Helps security teams assess and monitor the compliance posture of their own third-party vendors.

features.title: Broad integration ecosystem
features.description: Connects to AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, identity providers, ticketing tools, and version control systems.

pros: Continuous, automated monitoring reduces manual audit-prep work throughout the year
Broad framework coverage lets one evidence base support multiple certifications
Large, vetted auditor network streamlines running the actual audit
Trusted by well-known technology companies, reflecting strong enterprise credibility
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation, making budgeting harder upfront
Third-party estimates put annual costs from roughly 7,500 up to 100,000 dollars depending on scope, a wide range
Primarily suited to companies with a modern cloud stack; less turnkey for legacy on-premise environments
Multi-framework, multi-integration setups can require significant initial configuration effort

faqs.question: How much does Drata cost?
faqs.answer: Drata does not publish pricing publicly. Third-party estimates report tiers roughly from 7,500 dollars per year at the entry level to 15,000 dollars per year for a mid-tier plan, with custom pricing for larger, multi-framework customers.

faqs.question: Who founded Drata?
faqs.answer: Drata was founded in 2020 in San Diego, California, by Adam Markowitz, Daniel Marashlian, and Troy Markowitz.

faqs.question: What compliance frameworks does Drata support?
faqs.answer: Drata supports SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, PCI DSS, GDPR, and other common security and privacy frameworks.

faqs.question: How much funding has Drata raised?
faqs.answer: Drata has raised 328 million dollars across four rounds, including a 200 million dollar Series C in December 2022 at a 2 billion dollar valuation.

faqs.question: Does Drata help with the actual audit, not just evidence collection?
faqs.answer: Yes. Drata connects customers to a network of more than 2,500 vetted independent auditors who can review evidence directly within the platform.

faqs.question: What are Drata's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Drata's closest competitor is Vanta, with Secureframe, Sprinto, and Thoropass also competing in the compliance automation space.

use_cases.title: Achieving SOC 2 for the first time
use_cases.description: An early-stage SaaS startup uses Drata to collect evidence and pass its first SOC 2 Type II audit in order to close enterprise deals.

use_cases.title: Maintaining continuous compliance year-round
use_cases.description: A scale-up with existing certifications uses continuous monitoring to catch control drift immediately instead of scrambling before each audit renewal.

use_cases.title: Supporting multi-framework enterprise sales
use_cases.description: A growing company pursuing ISO 27001 and HIPAA alongside SOC 2 reuses a shared evidence base across all three frameworks to unblock enterprise contracts.

enrichment.short_description: Drawpile is a free, open-source collaborative drawing program that lets multiple people paint, sketch, and animate together in real time on the same canvas, with support for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and the web.
enrichment.company_size: Small, largely volunteer-driven project maintained under a German non-profit organization
enrichment.headquarters: Germany (Drawpile gemeinnützige UG, a non-profit organization)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Drawpile is a free, open-source app for real-time collaborative drawing and animation. See 2026 features, supported platforms, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Drawpile is a free and open-source collaborative drawing program that allows multiple people to paint, sketch, and animate together in real time on a single shared canvas, with each participant's cursor and brushstrokes visible to everyone in the session as they happen. The software traces its lineage back to earlier work by developer Calle Laakkonen, with the project as branded today dating from 2014, as reflected in the copyright notice on its official site ('© 2014-2026 Drawpile Contributors'). Development is primarily carried out by a small core team, historically centered on a single lead developer, with a broader community contributing code, translations, and user support. The project is legally organized under Drawpile gemeinnützige UG, a German non-profit corporate structure explicitly set up 'for the public good,' a governance choice designed to keep the code owned by the public and prevent the project from being taken proprietary.

Technically, Drawpile is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3) for its software, while the project's website content is separately licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0. It is available natively on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android, and a browser-based version extends access to platforms like iPad and iPhone without requiring installation. At its core, Drawpile functions as a networked painting application: a host starts or joins a drawing session on a server (either a self-hosted server or one of Drawpile's free official community servers), and every stroke made by any participant is transmitted and rendered live for everyone else in that session. Sessions can be left open to anyone or protected with a password to keep them private to invited collaborators, and a built-in chat function lets participants communicate while drawing.

On the creative-tooling side, Drawpile ships with more than 200 built-in brushes spanning multiple brush engines, along with a brush editor for building fully custom brushes, plus stroke stabilization to smooth shaky linework. It supports layers, layer groups, and blending modes for standard digital-painting workflows, along with shape tools, selections, transforms, and flood fill. Files can be exported to and imported from the OpenRaster (ORA) format as well as Photoshop's PSD format, easing interoperability with other digital art software. For animators, Drawpile includes a timeline with onion-skinning for frame-by-frame animation. More recent releases have added clipping layers, selection-masked brush strokes, OKLAB and spectral color painting modes, a redesigned selection action bar, and faster session reconnects. A distinctive resilience feature lets a user recover an interrupted session almost exactly where it left off if Drawpile crashes, if the operating system forces a reboot for updates, or if Android terminates the app in the background — replacing a traditional autosave mechanism. Drawpile also has a built-in timelapse recorder, accessible via the File menu's 'Make Timelapse' option, which can render a video of the entire drawing or animation process, optionally cropped to a specific region of the canvas.

Because Drawpile is free, open-source software with no company behind it, there is no pricing model, subscription, or paid tier: anyone can download the desktop or Android app at no cost, use the web version in a browser, and either self-host a session server or connect to one of the free official community servers that the project operates. This makes Drawpile distinct from most collaborative creative tools, which are typically commercial SaaS products with per-seat subscriptions.

Drawpile's target users are digital artists, art communities, classrooms, and hobbyists who want to draw together in real time — for collaborative illustration projects, live art battles or streams, teaching digital painting, or simply socializing while drawing with friends online. In the collaborative whiteboard and drawing space, it is often compared to tools such as Krita's (limited) collaborative features, Magma, or general-purpose whiteboard tools like Miro used informally for drawing, though those alternatives are either commercial or not purpose-built for real-time multi-user painting the way Drawpile is. Drawpile's core differentiation is that it is a dedicated, full-featured painting application with genuine real-time multi-user synchronization built in from the ground up, released as free software with governance structured specifically to keep it that way, rather than a commercial whiteboard tool with basic drawing functionality bolted on.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Drawpile is a free, open-source collaborative drawing and painting program that lets multiple users work on the same canvas simultaneously in real time.

The project, dating from 2014 under its current branding and rooted in earlier work by developer Calle Laakkonen, is legally organized under a German non-profit, Drawpile gemeinnützige UG, to keep the software permanently public and free.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: It includes over 200 built-in brushes, a custom brush editor, layers and blend modes, shape and selection tools, and support for OpenRaster and PSD file formats.
Animators get a timeline with onion-skinning, and every user benefits from a crash-recovery feature that restores a session almost exactly where it left off, plus a built-in timelapse recorder.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Drawpile is entirely free under the GPLv3 license, with no subscription, paid tier, or company behind it.
Users can self-host a session server or connect to free official community servers, and password-protect sessions to keep them private.

features.title: Real-time collaborative canvas
features.description: Multiple users see each other's cursors and brushstrokes live as they draw on the same canvas.

features.title: 200+ built-in brushes and a brush editor
features.description: Comes with a large brush library across multiple brush engines, plus tools to build fully custom brushes.

features.title: Layers, blend modes, and selections
features.description: Supports layers, layer groups, blending modes, shape tools, transforms, and flood fill for standard painting workflows.

features.title: Animation with onion skinning
features.description: A timeline and onion-skin feature enable rudimentary frame-by-frame animation.

features.title: Crash and session recovery
features.description: Recovers an interrupted drawing session almost exactly where it left off after a crash, forced reboot, or app termination.

features.title: Built-in timelapse recorder
features.description: Generates a video of the entire drawing or animation process, optionally cropped to a chosen canvas area.

features.title: OpenRaster and PSD support
features.description: Imports and exports files in the OpenRaster (ORA) format and Photoshop's PSD format for interoperability.

features.title: Password-protected private sessions
features.description: Sessions can be made public or locked with a password so only invited collaborators can join.

pros: Completely free and open source under the GPLv3 license
Genuine real-time multi-user drawing with no per-seat cost or account requirement
Available on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and via a browser-based version
Governed by a non-profit structure explicitly designed to keep the software public and free permanently
cons: Development is driven by a small team, so feature velocity can be slower than well-funded commercial tools
Lacks some advanced digital-painting features found in dedicated commercial apps like Photoshop or Clip Studio Paint
Real-time collaboration performance depends on the server used, whether self-hosted or a community server
Animation tools are described as rudimentary rather than a full animation production suite

faqs.question: Is Drawpile free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Drawpile is completely free, open-source software licensed under the GPLv3, with no subscription or paid tier.

faqs.question: What platforms does Drawpile support?
faqs.answer: Drawpile runs natively on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android, and has a browser-based version usable on devices like iPad and iPhone.

faqs.question: Who maintains Drawpile?
faqs.answer: Drawpile is maintained by a small core team under Drawpile gemeinnützige UG, a German non-profit organization, with contributions from a wider community.

faqs.question: Can I host my own Drawpile server?
faqs.answer: Yes. Users can self-host a session server or connect to Drawpile's free official community servers.

faqs.question: Does Drawpile support animation?
faqs.answer: Yes, Drawpile includes a timeline with onion-skinning for basic frame-by-frame animation.

faqs.question: What happens if Drawpile crashes mid-session?
faqs.answer: Drawpile can recover an interrupted session almost exactly where it left off, functioning as a replacement for traditional autosave.

use_cases.title: Real-time collaborative illustration
use_cases.description: A group of artists works together on a single piece of art in real time, seeing each other's brushstrokes live.

use_cases.title: Teaching digital painting
use_cases.description: An instructor demonstrates brush techniques live while students draw alongside them in the same session.

use_cases.title: Livestreamed art sessions and battles
use_cases.description: Streamers host live, multi-artist drawing sessions or friendly art battles that viewers can watch unfold in real time.

features: Access to free official community servers

enrichment.short_description: DrChrono is a cloud-based, mobile-first EHR, practice management, and medical billing platform for medical practices, originally built as one of the first iPad-native electronic health record systems.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom quote (reported range roughly $199-$600+/provider/month depending on tier)
enrichment.company_size: Part of EverCommerce (public company); mid-size team under the EverHealth solutions group
enrichment.headquarters: Sunnyvale/Mountain View, California, USA (now part of EverCommerce, headquartered in Denver, Colorado)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DrChrono is a cloud-based, mobile-first EHR and practice management platform. See 2026 pricing tiers, features, pros, cons, and alternatives for medical practices.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
DrChrono is a cloud-based electronic health record (EHR), practice management, and medical billing platform built for medical practices, best known for having launched in 2009 as one of the first EHR systems built natively for the iPad, at a time when most competitors were still tethered to desktop browsers or on-premise servers. The company was founded by Michael Nusimow and Daniel Kivatinos in California, with early operations based in the Mountain View and Sunnyvale area of Silicon Valley. DrChrono's mobile-first bet distinguished it early on: by building for tablets from day one rather than retrofitting a desktop product, it positioned itself to serve physicians who wanted to review charts and enter notes at the point of care rather than returning to a desktop workstation.

On November 2, 2021, EverCommerce Inc., a publicly traded vertical software company, announced a definitive agreement to acquire DrChrono, with the deal closing on November 18, 2021 for a reported price of roughly 180 million dollars, though EverCommerce did not disclose official financial terms at the time. Following the acquisition, DrChrono became part of EverCommerce's EverHealth solutions group, which bundles several healthcare-focused software products under one corporate umbrella. DrChrono has continued to operate under its own brand and product name since the acquisition, and the platform itself has kept expanding, most recently adding AI-driven tools such as automated medical speech-to-text and an AI clinical scribe product branded EverHealth Scribe.

Functionally, DrChrono combines several traditionally separate systems into one suite: an EHR for clinical documentation and charting, practice management tools for appointment scheduling and calendar management, revenue cycle management and medical billing (including claims submission), a patient portal for online scheduling and communication, and e-prescribing. It also offers a well-documented medical API that lets third-party developers and health-tech companies build integrations on top of DrChrono's platform, a differentiator that has made it popular among digital health startups that need EHR connectivity. The platform supports lab integrations, e-faxing, and both text and phone appointment reminders, with the specific volume of included texts, calls, and faxes varying by pricing tier.

DrChrono is sold in three tiers — Foundation, Growth, and Premium — priced per provider per month, though exact published pricing is not listed on its marketing site and prospective customers are directed to request a custom quote. Third-party pricing trackers report DrChrono's cost typically starting in the range of 199 to 250 dollars per provider per month for the Foundation tier, rising to roughly 300 to 600-plus dollars per provider per month for Growth and Premium plans that add more automated revenue cycle management workflows and AI-driven performance tools. Feature allotments scale with tier: for example, Foundation includes 250 text and 100 phone reminders per month along with 500 e-faxes, Growth doubles most of those allotments, and Premium goes further still with 1,000 texts, 750 calls, and 2,000 e-faxes, while all tiers include unlimited email reminders and staff accounts. Add-ons such as extended lab integration services (around 30 dollars per provider per month) and clinical speech-to-text or the EverHealth Scribe AI tool are available across tiers, though the AI scribe and speech-to-text tools are bundled into the Premium tier by default rather than sold separately.

DrChrono's core customers are solo practitioners, small and mid-size medical practices, and specialty clinics that want an all-in-one, cloud-based system rather than stitching together separate scheduling, billing, and charting software. Its most direct competitors include Kareo (also part of Tebra), athenahealth, eClinicalWorks, and, for larger health systems, Epic — though Epic and athenahealth generally target much larger enterprise and hospital-system deployments than DrChrono's typical small-practice customer base. DrChrono differentiates itself through its mobile- and tablet-first design heritage, its open, developer-friendly API, and, since joining EverCommerce, integration with EverHealth's broader suite of healthcare business tools, positioning it as a flexible, API-extensible EHR option for independent practices that value mobility and third-party integrations over the deep enterprise workflow customization that larger hospital-focused platforms offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DrChrono is a cloud-based EHR, practice management, and medical billing platform founded in 2009 by Michael Nusimow and Daniel Kivatinos as one of the first iPad-native electronic health record systems.

EverCommerce Inc. acquired DrChrono in November 2021 for a reported price around 180 million dollars, folding it into EverCommerce's EverHealth solutions group while keeping the DrChrono brand active.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform combines clinical charting, scheduling, revenue cycle management and medical billing, a patient portal, and e-prescribing into one suite, plus a developer-friendly medical API for third-party integrations.
Recent additions include AI-driven medical speech-to-text and the EverHealth Scribe AI clinical scribe tool, included by default in the top pricing tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: DrChrono sells three tiers — Foundation, Growth, and Premium — priced per provider per month, with exact figures available only via a custom quote.

Third-party pricing trackers report costs roughly from 199 to 250 dollars per provider per month at the Foundation level, rising to 300 to 600-plus dollars per provider per month for Growth and Premium plans with deeper billing automation and AI tools.

features.title: iPad-native, mobile-first EHR
features.description: Built from the outset for tablet use so clinicians can chart and review records at the point of care.

features.title: Integrated practice management
features.description: Appointment scheduling, calendar management, and staff workflows in the same system as clinical charting.

features.title: Revenue cycle management and billing
features.description: Automated claims submission and billing workflows, with more automation added at higher tiers.

features.title: Patient portal
features.description: Lets patients book appointments, message their provider, and access records online.

features.title: Developer-friendly medical API
features.description: A documented API lets third-party developers and health-tech companies build integrations on top of DrChrono.

features.title: E-prescribing and lab integrations
features.description: Supports electronic prescriptions and integration with lab services, with extended lab options available as an add-on.

features.title: Automated text, call, and fax reminders
features.description: Tiered monthly allotments of text messages, phone calls, and e-faxes to reduce patient no-shows and paperwork.

features.title: AI clinical scribe (EverHealth Scribe)
features.description: AI-driven medical speech-to-text and automated clinical note generation, included in the Premium tier.

pros: Strong mobile and tablet-first design heritage suited to point-of-care documentation
All-in-one suite covering EHR, billing, scheduling, and patient portal in a single system
Developer-friendly API makes it popular with health-tech integrators and startups
Now backed by EverCommerce's larger EverHealth group, adding financial stability and broader integrations
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a custom quote, making upfront cost comparison harder
Costs can rise significantly at Growth and Premium tiers, reported up to 600-plus dollars per provider per month
AI scribe and advanced speech-to-text tools are only included by default at the top Premium tier
Best suited to solo and small practices; larger health systems typically need more enterprise-focused platforms

faqs.question: How much does DrChrono cost?
faqs.answer: DrChrono requires a custom quote, but third-party pricing trackers report costs roughly from 199 to 250 dollars per provider per month for the Foundation tier, rising to 300 to 600-plus dollars for Growth and Premium.

faqs.question: Who founded DrChrono and when?
faqs.answer: DrChrono was founded in 2009 in California by Michael Nusimow and Daniel Kivatinos.

faqs.question: Is DrChrono still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No. EverCommerce Inc. acquired DrChrono in November 2021 for a reported price around 180 million dollars, and it now operates under EverCommerce's EverHealth solutions group.

faqs.question: Does DrChrono have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, DrChrono offers a documented medical API that lets third-party developers build integrations with the platform.

faqs.question: What pricing tiers does DrChrono offer?
faqs.answer: DrChrono offers three tiers: Foundation, Growth, and Premium, each with increasing feature allotments for reminders, e-faxes, and automation.

faqs.question: What are DrChrono's main competitors?
faqs.answer: DrChrono competes with Kareo/Tebra, athenahealth, eClinicalWorks, and, for larger health systems, Epic.

use_cases.title: Mobile charting for solo practitioners
use_cases.description: A solo physician uses DrChrono on an iPad to chart patient visits and e-prescribe directly at the point of care.

use_cases.title: All-in-one billing and scheduling
use_cases.description: A small practice consolidates scheduling, claims submission, and patient communication into one platform instead of separate tools.

use_cases.title: Building health-tech integrations
use_cases.description: A digital health startup uses DrChrono's medical API to connect its own application to practices' EHR data.

features: Core EHR, practice management, and billing
Unlimited email reminders and staff accounts
AI-driven performance tools and EverHealth Scribe included
